name: Digital Sovereignty Policy Lever
consequences: Immediate: Formal policy mandates reduce platform dependency → Systemic: 25% decrease in vendor concentration measured by dependency index → Strategic: Enhanced national resilience and bargaining power in procurement.
options: Maintain current reliance on Apple/Google with minimal policy adjustments
Introduce limited platform-neutral fallback mechanisms within existing procurement
Deploy a fully open-source, community-driven identity wallet ecosystem with blockchain-based trust anchors
review: Controls Supplier Concentration vs. Innovation Flexibility. Weakness: The options fail to consider the cost of transitioning existing citizen applications to new platforms.

name: Fallback Authentication Demonstration Lever
consequences: Immediate: Demonstrators prove feasibility of non-platform trust → Systemic: 40% increase in stakeholder confidence measured by survey score → Strategic: Accelerated policy adoption and procurement inclusion.
options: Develop a low-fidelity prototype using existing FIDO2 libraries on Android
Build a mid-fidelity demonstrator integrating GrapheneOS and browser-based fallback
Create a high-fidelity, cross-platform demonstrator leveraging quantum-resistant cryptography and decentralized identity protocols
review: Controls Technical Credibility vs. Development Risk. Weakness: The options overlook the need for regulatory certification pathways.

name: Procurement Neutrality Lever
consequences: Immediate: Procurement documents include neutrality clauses → Systemic: 15% reduction in procurement cycle time measured by days to award → Strategic: Faster market entry for alternative vendors and reduced lock-in.
options: Add a single clause requiring alternative app distribution channels
Mandate a dual-channel distribution requirement in procurement specifications
Implement a dynamic procurement framework that automatically reallocates budget to emerging open-source vendors
review: Controls Vendor Lock-in vs. Market Competition. Weakness: The options fail to address the potential increase in administrative overhead for procurement teams.

name: EU Standards Alignment Lever
consequences: Immediate: Alignment with EU Digital Identity Wallet standards → Systemic: 20% increase in cross-border interoperability measured by API compatibility score → Strategic: Strengthened Denmark’s influence in EU policy and reduced regulatory friction.
options: Submit technical comments to existing EU working groups
Lead a joint working group on platform-neutral identity standards
Establish a pan-European open-source identity federation powered by zero-knowledge proofs
review: Controls National Autonomy vs. EU Integration. Weakness: The options ignore the impact on Denmark’s domestic regulatory compliance costs.

name: Stakeholder Coalition Lever
consequences: Immediate: Expanded coalition increases advocacy reach → Systemic: 30% rise in public support measured by petition signatures → Strategic: Greater political leverage to enforce policy changes.
options: Maintain current coalition of civil society and academia
Expand coalition to include industry and private sector stakeholders
Form a transnational alliance with other Nordic countries to create a unified platform-neutral identity strategy
review: Controls Political Momentum vs. Coalition Cohesion. Weakness: The options fail to consider the risk of divergent national interests within a transnational alliance.

name: Digital Identity Resilience Architecture Lever
consequences: Immediate: Introduce modular trust layers → Systemic: 30% reduction in single-point failure risk measured by uptime metrics → Strategic: Greater flexibility for future platform shifts.
options: Maintain the existing monolithic architecture with minor redundancy enhancements
Add a secondary verification channel using hardware tokens
Deploy a decentralized identity ledger using blockchain-based verifiable credentials
review: Controls Monolithic Architecture vs Decentralized Resilience. Weakness: The options fail to consider the cost of retraining citizen-facing services.

name: Citizen Access Pathway Lever
consequences: Immediate: Provide alternative login flows → Systemic: 20% increase in authentication success rate measured by login logs → Strategic: Higher citizen trust and reduced support tickets.
options: Maintain the current SMS-based OTP fallback
Add a QR-code based web login with device pairing
Deploy a biometric enclave using on-device secure enclaves and federated learning
review: Controls Convenience vs Security. Weakness: The options fail to address cross-device usability consistency.

name: Regulatory Incentive Lever
consequences: Immediate: Introduce procurement incentives for platform-neutral solutions → Systemic: 15% increase in vendor diversity measured by number of approved vendors → Strategic: Accelerated market entry for open-source identity stacks.
options: Maintain a small grant for pilot projects
Add tax credits for compliance with neutrality standards
Deploy a public procurement marketplace powered by AI-driven vendor scoring
review: Controls Incentive Complexity vs Vendor Diversity. Weakness: The options overlook the administrative burden of new compliance reporting.

name: Innovation Ecosystem Lever
consequences: Immediate: Foster a startup incubator for identity tech → Systemic: 25% growth in local identity startups measured by funding rounds → Strategic: Build a domestic talent pipeline and reduce reliance on foreign expertise.
options: Maintain a hackathon with limited prizes
Add a seed fund for vetted identity startups
Deploy a distributed ledger-based accelerator with tokenized equity stakes
review: Controls Talent Development vs Resource Allocation. Weakness: The options ignore the need for sustained mentorship and regulatory guidance.

name: Cross-Border Collaboration Lever
consequences: Immediate: Align Danish identity standards with Nordic peers → Systemic: 10% increase in cross-border authentication success measured by intergovernmental data exchange logs → Strategic: Strengthened regional resilience and shared procurement savings.
options: Maintain a memorandum of understanding with neighboring countries
Add a joint technical working group on interoperability
Deploy a pan-Nordic identity federation using quantum-resistant protocols
review: Controls National Focus vs Regional Integration. Weakness: The options fail to consider differing national legal frameworks.

name: Identity Supply Chain Diversification Lever
consequences: Immediate: Expand vendor base for identity components → Systemic: 35% increase in supply‑chain redundancy measured by number of alternative certification bodies → Strategic: Reduced single‑point failure risk and enhanced procurement leverage.
options: Maintain current reliance on a single certification authority
Add a secondary national certification body to the identity supply chain
Create a multi‑stakeholder consortium that certifies identity components using blockchain‑based attestation
review: Controls Vendor Concentration vs. Certification Complexity. Weakness: The options ignore the cost of aligning legacy systems with new certification standards.

name: Citizen Trust Assurance Lever
consequences: Immediate: Strengthen public confidence in digital identity → Systemic: 25% rise in citizen satisfaction measured by survey response rate → Strategic: Higher adoption rates and reduced support burden.
options: Issue a public information campaign highlighting existing security measures
Publish a third‑party audit of current identity infrastructure
Launch a citizen‑centric transparency portal powered by zero‑knowledge proof dashboards
review: Controls Transparency vs. Operational Overhead. Weakness: The options fail to address the need for continuous audit updates.

name: Technology Adoption Acceleration Lever
consequences: Immediate: Accelerate deployment of emerging authentication tech → Systemic: 20% reduction in time‑to‑market for new identity features measured by release cycle length → Strategic: Competitive advantage in digital service delivery.
options: Adopt incremental updates to existing FIDO2 libraries
Integrate a hybrid biometric‑token solution using secure enclaves
Deploy a quantum‑resistant, decentralized identity protocol with token‑based governance
review: Controls Innovation Speed vs. Security Assurance. Weakness: The options overlook the regulatory readiness for quantum‑resistant standards.

name: Governance Transparency Lever
consequences: Immediate: Increase visibility of identity governance processes → Systemic: 30% improvement in stakeholder engagement measured by meeting attendance → Strategic: Stronger accountability and reduced political risk.
options: Publish quarterly governance reports on identity operations
Establish an open‑source governance dashboard for policy decisions
Create a decentralized autonomous organization (DAO) that governs identity standards via token voting
review: Controls Accountability vs. Decision‑making Efficiency. Weakness: The options fail to consider the legal enforceability of DAO‑based decisions.

name: Resilience Funding Lever
consequences: Immediate: Secure dedicated resilience budget → Systemic: 40% increase in contingency funds measured by budget allocation percentage → Strategic: Greater financial flexibility for rapid response to platform disruptions.
options: Allocate a fixed percentage of the national digital budget to resilience initiatives
Create a resilience reserve fund backed by sovereign bonds
Establish a public‑private partnership that invests in resilience through tokenized assets
review: Controls Budget Allocation vs. Investment Risk. Weakness: The options ignore the potential dilution of existing digital service funding.
